summary.text: Clear and {temp}. Full sun, but cold air; dress like it's colder than it looks.
{temp} under a cloudless sky. Clear nights lose heat fast, so it cools quickly after sunset.
Sunny and {temp} today. The sun's up, but its low winter angle keeps the warmth limited.
Clear all day at {temp}. A coat helps more than the blue sky suggests.
{temp}, clear skies. Bright light and cold air, a common pairing this time of year.
Clear and {temp}. Good visibility, low temperatures; layers you can shed indoors work best.
Cloudless and {temp} today. Direct sun alone won't do much for the temperature.
High of {high}, low of {low} today. Clear skies help by day and hurt after sunset.
Clear and {temp}. A comfortable day to be outside for a while.
{temp} and clear all day. Good conditions for anything you had planned outdoors.
Blue sky, {temp}. One of the more pleasant stretches of weather this week.
Clear skies and {temp}. Comfortable enough to skip a jacket for most of the day.
{temp} under full sun. Steady, easy weather with nothing to plan around.
Clear and {temp}. Good light most of the day, cooling a little after sunset.
Sunny and {temp}. A dependable day, weather-wise.
Clear through the evening, {temp}. Comfortable enough for a walk after dinner.
High of {high}, low of {low} today. A wide, comfortable range for the whole day.
Clear and {temp}. Full sun all day, so plan for shade during the afternoon peak.
Sunny and {temp}. No cloud cover to soften direct sun, so sunscreen matters more today.
{temp} under a cloudless sky. Hydration and shade go a long way today.
Clear all day at {temp}. Heat builds through the afternoon with nothing to blunt it.
{temp}, not a cloud in sight. Outdoor plans suit morning or evening better than midday.
Clear and {temp}. Close to the seasonal ceiling for this area.
{temp} and sunny. UV exposure runs highest under skies this clear.
Feels like {feelsLike}, actual {temp}. Humidity is adding several degrees to how it feels.
Clear skies, {temp}. Worth front-loading errands before midday heat sets in.
Cloudy and {temp}. The cloud layer is actually helping, trapping a little warmth overnight.
{temp} under a gray sky. No sun breaks expected; temperatures hold fairly steady.
Overcast and {temp}. Flat light today, but the clouds keep it from getting much colder.
Cloudy skies, {temp}. A stable, unremarkable day, temperature-wise.
{temp}, clouds holding steady. Little change expected through the day.
Gray and {temp}. Cloud cover all day, with no clearing in the forecast.
Cloud cover, {temp}. Overcast nights like this often trap more heat than clear ones.
{temp} and overcast. Flat light, steady temperatures.
Cloudy and {temp}. Comfortable temperatures, just without much direct sun.
{temp} under clouds. Good conditions for being outside, even without sunshine.
Overcast and {temp}. Mild and steady, with no real surprises in the forecast.
Gray skies, {temp}. Comfortable weather, just filtered light.
{temp}, mostly clouds. A calm, easy day.
Cloudy at {temp}. Nothing dramatic, comfortable temperatures throughout.
Overcast, {temp}. A dependable, mild day.
{temp} and cloudy. Steady conditions from morning through evening.
{chance} chance of rain later. Thick enough cloud cover that a shower is plausible; worth carrying an umbrella.
Rain's a {chance} chance today. Keep an umbrella nearby in case the clouds tip over.
Cloudy but {temp}. Cloud cover isn't cutting the heat much today.
{temp} under cloud cover. Humidity is likely doing more to how it feels than the clouds are.
Overcast and {temp}. Cloud cover today without much cooling effect.
Gray skies, {temp} anyway. Heat is building despite the lack of direct sun.
{temp}, mostly clouds. Humid air under cloud cover tends to feel heavier.
Cloudy at {temp}. Warm and humid, with the cloud layer holding moisture close to the ground.
Overcast, {temp}. Lower sunburn risk today, but the underlying heat is unchanged.
{temp} and cloudy. Clouds and cooler air don't always go together, and today's an example.
Rain and {temp}. Cold rain soaks through faster than warm rain; waterproof layers matter today.
{temp} with rain coming down. This combination tends to feel colder than the number alone suggests.
Rain, {temp}. A sturdy umbrella and a warm layer underneath handle this well.
{temp} and wet. Rain at this temperature chills quickly.
Rain at {temp}. Wet and cold together pull body heat away faster than either alone.
Cold rain, {temp}. Good conditions for staying dry and layered rather than braving it.
{temp}, steady rain. No thunder in this one, just a long, soaking rain.
Rain and {temp}. Even a short walk outside calls for real rain gear today.
Rain moving in around {time}. Plan outdoor time before then if you can.
{temp} and raining. Comfortable temperatures, just wet; an umbrella covers most of it.
Rain, {temp}. Mild enough that a light jacket and umbrella are all this needs.
{temp} with showers. Steady rain rather than anything severe.
Raining, {temp}. An ordinary rainy day, nothing more.
{temp} and wet out. Mild temperatures mean the rain won't feel especially cold.
Showers expected by {time}. The rest of the day should stay dry.
{temp}, steady rain. Bring a jacket; the temperature itself isn't the issue today.
Rain at {temp}. Warm rain like this raises humidity rather than lowering it.
{temp} and raining. The added moisture makes the air feel heavier than the temperature alone suggests.
Warm rain, {temp}. It won't cool things down much.
Rain, {temp} out. Humidity climbs when rain falls into already-warm air.
{temp} with showers. Expect visible steam or mist rising off warm pavement as it rains.
Raining and {temp}. The air stays humid through the rain, not just after it.
{temp}, wet out. A warm shower like this cools the ground more than the air.
Rain at {temp}. Humidity stays high even with rain falling.
{temp} but feels like {feelsLike}. The rain is adding humidity, not relief.
Snow and {temp}. Cold enough that snow will stick and accumulate rather than melt.
{temp} and snowing. Typical conditions for accumulating snow this time of year.
Snow, {temp}. Cold pavement means snow settles quickly rather than melting on contact.
{temp} with snow coming down. Give yourself extra time; roads may need clearing before you head out.
Snowing at {temp}. Temperatures well below freezing mean this snow isn't going anywhere fast.
{temp}, steady snow. A good day to check road conditions before heading out later.
Snow and {temp}. Cold enough for accumulation to build through the day.
{temp}, snow falling. Typical winter conditions for the season.
High of {high} today, low of {low} tonight. Bundle up; temperatures stay well below freezing.
Snow at {temp}. Warm enough that it likely won't stick around long.
{temp} with snow falling. Expect it to melt on contact given the temperature.
Snow, {temp} out. This close to freezing, snow and light rain can trade places quickly.
{temp}, light snow. Accumulation is unlikely at this temperature.
Snowing at {temp}. The ground isn't cold enough for much of it to stick.
{temp} with flurries. Could easily turn to rain if temperatures tick up slightly.
Snow at {temp}. A borderline temperature for snow to hold.
{temp}, snow that may not last. Watch for it turning to rain later.
Snow in the forecast at {temp}. An unusual combination worth double-checking closer to the time.
{temp} with snow showing in the data. Uncommon for this temperature range.
Snow, {temp} out. A rare pairing; elevation or timing differences can explain readings like this.
{temp}, snow appearing in the forecast. Worth confirming with a closer look before relying on it.
Snow at {temp}. Uncommon, but the forecast data is reporting it as-is.
{temp} with snow forecast. Worth checking again as the timing gets closer.
Snow reported at {temp}. An unusual reading; treat it as provisional until it's nearer.
{temp}, snow on the forecast. Not typical for this temperature.
Foggy out there. Visibility is reduced, especially on the roads.
Fog this morning. It typically burns off as the sun warms the air later.
Foggy conditions. Visibility can drop to a few hundred feet or less in dense patches.
Fog rolling through. Headlights on and extra following distance help today.
Foggy out. Visibility is the main thing to plan around, not temperature.
Fog today. Usually clears by midmorning as temperatures rise.
Foggy conditions. Nothing's changed on the ground, just how far you can see it.
Fog out there. Slow down on the roads until it lifts.
Windy out there. Loose outdoor items are worth securing today.
Breezy today. Strong enough to notice, not strong enough for a warning.
Windy. Expect some sway in trees and power lines, nothing severe.
Breezy out. Good day to check that nothing's loose on a porch or balcony.
Windy today. Cycling and umbrellas are both harder than usual.
Gusty out there. Umbrellas tend to turn inside out in gusts like this.
Windy. Flags and branches are visibly moving; sustained gusts are likely.
Breezy today. Steady wind rather than short gusts.
Thunderstorms today. Expect lightning and heavy rain in bursts rather than all day.
Storms rolling in. Indoor plans are the safer call while they pass through.
Thunder and rain. Loud but typically brief, passing within an hour or two.
Storms today. Short-lived but intense while they last.
Thunderstorms in the forecast. Unplugging sensitive electronics is a reasonable precaution.
Storms coming through. Best watched from indoors, away from windows during the worst of it.
Thunder expected. Conditions can turn quickly once storms develop.
Storms today. Brief but capable of heavy rain and lightning while active.
Today's forecast is in. Details above.
Full conditions for today are shown above.
Today's numbers are in; temperature, conditions, and the rest are above.
Forecast loaded. See above for today's specifics.

skyPlanet.text: Mercury is out tonight, low near the horizon just after sunset; it never strays far from the Sun's glare.
Mercury's visible for a narrow window tonight, close to the horizon, before it sets shortly after the Sun.
Mercury tonight: the closest planet to the Sun, which is why it only ever shows up briefly at dawn or dusk.
Mercury's up tonight. It orbits the Sun in just 88 days, the fastest of any planet.
Mercury tonight, low on the horizon. Being this close to the Sun makes it one of the harder planets to catch.
Mercury's showing tonight; look right after sunset, low in the west, before it drops below the horizon.
Mercury's up tonight, never far from the Sun in the sky, which is why it's only visible in twilight.
Mercury's visible briefly tonight, close to where the Sun just set.
Venus is out tonight, the brightest point in the sky after the Moon, and hard to mistake for a star.
Venus tonight, unmissable: it reflects more sunlight than any other planet, which is why it outshines everything nearby.
Venus is up, brighter than any star in the sky. Second planet from the Sun, first in brightness.
Venus tonight. If you can only find one planet with the naked eye, this is usually it.
Venus is visible, often called the evening star, though it's a planet, not a star at all.
Venus, steady and brilliant tonight. Its thick cloud cover reflects sunlight exceptionally well.
Venus tonight, the brightest point you'll see low in the sky after sunset.
Venus is up there, as bright as it gets; nothing else in the evening sky competes with it.
Mars is out tonight, with a faint rust-colored glow; the color comes from iron oxide dust on its surface.
Mars tonight: steady and reddish, and unlike stars, planets don't twinkle.
Mars is visible tonight. Not the brightest object up there, but its reddish tint makes it easy to identify.
Mars tonight, holding a quiet orange-red point, the same iron-rich dust that gives it its nickname, the Red Planet.
Mars is up there tonight, completing its own slow orbit roughly every 687 days.
Mars tonight, modest in brightness but distinctive in color.
Mars is out. Look for the steady reddish point that doesn't flicker the way stars do.
Mars tonight, easy to pick out by color even though it isn't especially bright.
Jupiter is out tonight, the second-brightest planet in the sky and the largest in the solar system.
Jupiter tonight, bright and steady, hard to miss even without binoculars.
Jupiter is visible. It's the biggest planet in the solar system, more than 11 times Earth's diameter.
Jupiter's up there, steady and bright, light that's taken over 30 minutes to reach Earth tonight.
Jupiter tonight: a decent pair of binoculars can reveal its four largest moons as tiny points of light nearby.
Jupiter is out, consistently one of the brightest objects in the night sky.
Jupiter tonight, an easy target for a first attempt at spotting a planet.
Jupiter's visible tonight, not as bright as Venus, but far larger and further away.
Saturn is out tonight. The rings won't show without a telescope, but the planet itself is visible as a steady point.
Saturn tonight, a modest gold-toned point of light; its famous rings need at least a small telescope to see.
Saturn is visible tonight, though its rings are invisible to the naked eye; binoculars barely hint at them.
Saturn's up there tonight, dimmer than Jupiter but still steady and identifiable.
Saturn tonight, pale gold and easy to confuse with a star at a glance.
Saturn is out. It looks like an ordinary star to the naked eye; the rings only appear through a telescope.
Saturn tonight, moving through its roughly 29-year orbit around the Sun.
Saturn's visible tonight. Its rings are real and enormous, just too fine a detail for unaided eyes.

skyNoPlanets.text: No naked-eye planets are up tonight; their orbits simply haven't lined up with a clear view from here.
Nothing planetary is visible tonight. Planet visibility depends on where each one sits in its orbit relative to Earth and the Sun.
No planets are in view tonight. Check back tomorrow; their positions shift slightly every night.
None of the five naked-eye planets are up tonight. The stars are still there to look at.
No planet sightings tonight; this happens periodically as planets pass too close to the Sun's glare to see.
Tonight's naked-eye planet count is zero, simply due to current orbital positions.
No planets are visible tonight. The rest of the night sky is unaffected.
Zero visible planets tonight. Orbital timing varies night to night, so this changes regularly.

skyAurora.text: No aurora expected tonight; the Sun has been quiet this week, so there isn't enough solar wind to trigger one.
Aurora forecast: none tonight. Geomagnetic activity is low, which is what auroras depend on.
No aurora tonight. Auroras need a disturbance in Earth's magnetic field, and none is expected.
No aurora expected tonight; the Sun's current activity level is too low to spark one.
Aurora odds are essentially zero tonight. A calm solar wind means nothing to light up the sky.
No northern lights expected tonight. The geomagnetic conditions simply aren't there.
Aurora: unlikely tonight, tied to the Sun's currently low activity.
Nothing auroral expected tonight; the Sun has had a quiet week overall.
Aurora: a faint outside chance tonight, and even then, only low on the northern horizon.
Slight aurora possibility tonight; geomagnetic activity is elevated just enough to matter a little.
Aurora: low odds tonight. Worth a glance north if you're already outside, not a special trip.
A modest aurora chance tonight, tied to a minor uptick in geomagnetic activity.
Aurora: unlikely but not impossible tonight, given the current activity level.
Low aurora activity forecast tonight; a real possibility, just a small one.
Aurora odds are thin tonight. Even if it appears, expect a faint glow near the horizon, not overhead color.
Aurora: a near miss tonight, per the current geomagnetic forecast.
Aurora: fair chance tonight, worth a look if you're already outside and away from city lights.
Fair aurora odds tonight; a genuine, if modest, chance near the northern horizon.
Aurora: a decent chance tonight. Find a dark spot and give your eyes a few minutes to adjust.
Fair chance of aurora tonight, tied to moderately elevated geomagnetic activity.
Aurora tonight: fair odds, most visible low on the horizon rather than overhead.
Fair aurora chance tonight; not guaranteed, but a real possibility.
Aurora: fair tonight, a reasonable reason to look north for a few minutes.
Tonight's aurora odds are fair, better than an average night, with no guarantee.
Aurora: good chance tonight; this one's worth stepping outside for.
Good aurora odds tonight, tied to a notable rise in geomagnetic activity.
Aurora tonight: good chance, with a real possibility of color higher in the sky, not just near the horizon.
Good chance of aurora tonight; worth checking a few times over the evening.
Aurora: good odds tonight, uncommon enough to be worth a short trip somewhere darker.
Tonight's aurora forecast is good; a stronger night than most.
Aurora: good chance tonight, with activity levels higher than a typical night.
Good aurora activity expected tonight, worth the walk to somewhere dark if you can.
Aurora: strong activity expected tonight; a rare, genuinely notable display is possible.
Strong aurora tonight, likely visible well overhead rather than just as a horizon glow.
Aurora: strong tonight, tied to significant geomagnetic activity. Worth staying up for.
Tonight's aurora forecast is strong; among the best conditions this app will show all year.
Strong aurora activity tonight, the kind worth driving somewhere dark for.
Aurora: strong tonight, genuinely notable. Look up, not just toward the northern horizon.
Strong geomagnetic activity tonight; aurora odds are about as good as they get.
Aurora: strong tonight, a rare night worth telling people about before it happens.

skyISSPass.text: The ISS passes over tonight; it crosses the sky in a few minutes, looking like a bright star moving steadily, with no blinking and no sound.
Station's overhead tonight, orbiting at about 17,500 mph roughly 250 miles up.
The ISS is due over tonight. It reflects sunlight, which is why it's visible even though it has no lights of its own.
ISS pass tonight; it moves in a smooth, steady line across the sky, unlike a plane's blinking lights or a star's fixed position.
The space station crosses tonight, taking just a few minutes to travel from horizon to horizon.
ISS overhead tonight, home to a full crew, moving fast enough to circle the Earth every 90 minutes.
The ISS swings by tonight, among the brightest and fastest-moving objects visible to the naked eye.
Tonight's ISS pass is brief: a steady bright point crossing the sky in a few minutes, then gone.

skyNoISS.text: No visible ISS pass tonight; its orbit just doesn't bring it overhead in daylight-lit conditions from here right now.
The station is orbiting elsewhere tonight. Its path shifts by a few degrees with each 90-minute orbit.
No ISS pass tonight; visibility depends on the station being lit by the Sun while the sky here is dark, and tonight the timing doesn't line up.
Nothing overhead from the station tonight. Check back; its ground track changes daily.
No ISS visibility tonight. It orbits the Earth roughly every 90 minutes regardless, just not over this location right now.
The ISS isn't visible tonight; a matter of orbital geometry, not a change in the station itself.
No station pass tonight. It circles the planet continuously; the next visible pass will come as its orbit shifts.
ISS: no pass tonight. Another location is getting the current viewing window instead.

skyMoon.text: New moon tonight; the Moon sits between Earth and the Sun, so its near side isn't lit at all from here.
New moon: the darkest sky of the month, which makes it the best night for spotting faint stars and the Milky Way.
New moon tonight. It isn't visible, not because anything's wrong, but because sunlight is hitting its far side.
New moon: the darkest sky of the lunar cycle. Good conditions for stargazing without moonlight washing things out.
New moon tonight; nothing to see of the Moon itself, but the darkest skies of the month for everything else.
New moon: the Moon is up during the day and sets with the Sun, invisible against the daytime sky.
New moon tonight, a good night to look for fainter stars that moonlight usually drowns out.
New moon: the start of a roughly 29.5-day cycle back to the next new moon.
Waxing moon tonight; a bit more illuminated than last night, on its way toward full.
The Moon is waxing tonight, growing a little each night as it moves from new toward full.
Waxing moon tonight, roughly halfway or less through its 29.5-day cycle.
The Moon's on its way to full tonight, growing steadily night by night.
Waxing tonight; the illuminated portion increases each night until the Moon reaches full.
The Moon is growing tonight, the first half of its monthly cycle from new to full.
Waxing moon tonight, visible for a longer stretch each evening as it brightens.
The Moon is filling out tonight, a predictable stage on its way to full.
Full moon tonight; the entire near side is lit, making it the brightest the night sky gets without help.
Full moon tonight. It's bright enough to see by outdoors, even without other light sources.
Full moon tonight, the point in its cycle where the Sun, Earth, and Moon roughly line up.
Full moon tonight; fully illuminated and visible essentially all night, rising around sunset.
Full moon tonight, the brightest natural light in the night sky.
Full moon tonight. Its gravity has a measurable effect on ocean tides, at its strongest around now.
Full moon tonight, lighting the sky enough to wash out all but the brightest stars nearby.
Full moon tonight, round and fully lit from horizon to horizon.
Waning moon tonight, a little less illuminated than yesterday as it heads back toward new.
The Moon is waning tonight, past its full point and shrinking night by night.
Waning moon tonight, the second half of its roughly 29.5-day cycle.
The Moon's illuminated portion is shrinking tonight, on its way back to new.
Waning tonight; this is the stretch of the month with progressively darker skies each night.
The Moon is tapering off tonight, the mirror image of its earlier waxing phase.
Waning moon tonight, still visible but illuminated less each night.
The Moon is heading back toward new tonight, shrinking steadily as it goes.

skyMeteor.text: The {shower} are near peak tonight, and with little moonlight, expect close to their full advertised rate.
Dark sky, active shower; close to the best conditions meteor-watching gets.
The Moon is mostly absent tonight, so faint {shower} meteors that would otherwise wash out should be visible.
No real moonlight tonight; the best chance the {shower} get to show their full rate.
The sky is dark and the {shower} are active tonight; worth the late night if you can manage it.
Moon's out of the way tonight, which matters more for a meteor shower than clear skies alone.
The {shower} peak under a properly dark sky tonight, with no moonlight cutting into the count.
Clear, dark conditions with no moon competing. The {shower} get the whole sky tonight.
The {shower} are active tonight, though some moonlight is cutting into how many you'll see.
Partial moonlight tonight; the {shower} are still worth a look, just at a somewhat reduced rate.
The Moon is up for part of the night, trimming the {shower} count during that stretch.
Some glow from the Moon tonight; the fainter {shower} meteors won't all make it through.
The {shower} are peaking, with the Moon's brightness working against the fainter meteors.
Moonlight is taking a modest bite out of tonight's {shower} count. Still active, just reduced.
The {shower} are up there tonight; the Moon is bright enough to matter, not enough to erase it.
A partly-lit sky tonight; the {shower} are active, with rates lower than a moonless night.
The bright moon will wash out fainter meteors tonight; expect closer to a handful an hour, and look away from the moon.
Tonight's {shower} numbers are real on paper, but a bright Moon will cut deeply into what's actually visible.
A bright Moon is significantly reducing visible {shower} meteors tonight; expect only the brightest ones.
The {shower} are active tonight, but heavy moonlight will wash out most of the fainter meteors.
The Moon is near full or close to it tonight, which sharply reduces how many {shower} meteors you'll actually see.
Technically tonight is the {shower} peak, but strong moonlight will hide all but the brightest streaks; face away from the Moon for the best chance.
The {shower} are up there tonight, but you'll be watching under a very bright Moon; expect a much lower rate than the shower's peak number.
Bright moonlight tonight is washing out most of the {shower}; only the brightest fireballs are likely to cut through.

skyPairing.text: Two bright objects share a small patch of sky tonight; a conjunction, where they appear close from our line of sight even though they're vastly far apart.
Tonight's pairing puts two bright objects side by side in the sky; an alignment of positions, not actual proximity in space.
A close pairing tonight: two objects that happen to line up as seen from Earth, though they're nowhere near each other in reality.
Tonight's pairing won't repeat in exactly this configuration for a while; worth a glance while it lasts.
Two objects share one line of sight tonight, close enough together to take in with the naked eye, no telescope needed.
Tonight's pairing is a conjunction; an apparent closeness caused by orbital timing, not any real connection between the two.
Nothing physically connects these two, but tonight they'll appear side by side from where you're standing.
A tight pairing tonight, close enough that the two objects will be visible together in the same small area of sky.

skyLaunch.text: Launch dates often shift due to weather, hardware checks, or range availability; treat the listed date as the current best estimate.
A launch window is the specific time range during which a rocket can lift off to reach its intended orbit.
Scrubs, or postponed launches, are common; a delay doesn't mean a mission is in trouble.
Countdown holds can be scheduled or unscheduled; either way, they're a normal part of most launches.
T-0 marks the moment of liftoff; times before that are counting down, not the launch itself.
Launch schedules update frequently as providers firm up weather and technical readiness closer to the date.
A launch can be delayed by hours or by weeks; the reasons range from weather to last-minute technical checks.
Liftoff itself takes seconds; the review process behind each scheduled date takes considerably longer.

skySolar.text: The Sun is having a quiet week; solar activity is low, with no notable flares.
No flares worth mentioning right now. Solar activity is at a seasonal or cyclical low.
Quiet sun, quiet skies; low solar activity means minimal effect on radio or satellites.
Nothing on the Sun is generating notable activity right now.
Solar activity: low. Radios, satellites, and GPS remain unaffected.
The Sun is calm right now; sunspot activity is minimal.
A calm stretch on the Sun; sunspots are present, but flare activity is low.
Solar activity is quiet today, roughly 93 million miles away.
An M-class flare occurred recently; moderate in strength, enough to cause minor radio interference.
The Sun is a bit more active than usual. Radios may pick up brief static, nothing more.
Some flaring today, moderate in scale. Satellites and GPS are largely unaffected at this level.
Activity is up a notch; moderate solar weather that mostly shows up in radio data, not everyday effects.
A moderate flare came and went. HF radio operators may have noticed a brief disruption.
The Sun is more active than its recent baseline, though not at storm level.
Elevated activity today; worth noting, though not disruptive to most day-to-day technology.
A moderate event occurred on the Sun recently, within the range considered normal, elevated activity.
An X-class flare peaked recently; the strongest category, capable of disrupting HF radio and some GPS signals.
Geomagnetic storm levels are elevated. Radio blackouts and GPS accuracy issues are possible.
The Sun had a genuinely disruptive event. Expect potential radio static and reduced GPS precision.
Storm-level activity is happening right now; real effects on radio communication and navigation systems.
This is an active solar storm. Radio and GPS systems may be affected for a period.
Strong flare activity today. HF radio blackouts are a realistic possibility during this window.
The Sun is producing the kind of activity that can affect polar flight routes and satellite operations.
High storm levels right now; strong enough to cause measurable disruption to radio and GPS systems.
